sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Minor wound management aims to control bleeding, reduce contamination, protect deeper structures, and hand the patient over with a wound that is clean and safely dressed. In prehospital care, the focus is usually preparation and escalation, not definitive closure of every wound.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Control bleeding first with direct pressure

2. Expose and inspect the wound for contamination, depth, tendon or joint involvement, foreign body risk, and distal neurovascular status

3. Irrigate thoroughly when appropriate and safe to do so

4. Apply a suitable dressing and support the injured area

5. Use temporary edge approximation only for simple, clean, low-tension wounds within scope

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Dirty, bite, crush, puncture, or delayed wounds usually need cleaning and referral rather than closure
- Scalp wounds can bleed dramatically and still be manageable with direct pressure and dressing alone
- Think about tetanus status, retained foreign body, and deeper structure injury even in apparently minor wounds
- Document the mechanism, wound features, dressing, and escalation advice
